english: I. adj. putrid, rancid, rotten. II. vb., pf. bsúms? (bsums) fut. bsum (bsum), Sch. to bind or tie together, to draw together; to condense.
Pers. سُرنا, hautboy, larger than the glíṅ-bu (gling bu) and sounding sharper; for profane use.
v. su-ru-pán-tsá (su ru pan tsa).
Med., colocynth.
coarse-grained, e.g. grits W.

an artificial plait or gather made in a dress W.; furrow, channel, groove, trench, ditch (Cs.); ri-súl (ri sul) lateral valley, ravine, hollow, ri-súl-gyi groṅ-k̇yér (ri sul gyi grong khyer) ravine as a haunt of evil spirits; brag-sul (brag sul) narrow ravine between rocks; ka-súl (ka sul) the fluting in a column; súl-ċan (sul can) furrowed, having plaits or folds; súl-ma (sul ma) an angular, not round, vessel; sul-mál (sul mal) the third stomach of ruminating animals, the psalterium or book-tripe Sch., sul-máṅs (sul mangs) Lt.
1. instr. of su (su). - 2. k̇yeu-sús (khye'u sus) Gyatch. ལ, 13, also Stg.?
1. Ld. inst. of te (te) after s (s), e.g. *zós-se* (*zos se*) - 2. num.: 118.

1. snapping one's fingers. - 2. the time it takes to do this, i.e. a very short time, a moment, a twinkling Cs., se-gól-gyi sgra (se gol gyi sgra) the sound produced by snapping the fingers, se-gól-gyi brda (se gol gyi brda) a signal given by it Cs.; se-gól ytóg-pa (se gol ytog pa) Mil., byéd-pa (byed pa) Mil., brdáb-pa (brdab pa) Glr. to snap one's fingers as a sign of contempt or indignation.
yellow beads of a rosary, coming from the central part of Tibet, accounted more valuable than *rág-ša* (*rag sha*) W.
v. se-šiṅ (se shing).
the disagreeable smell of the sweat of the arm-pits Lt., se-dri bsnám-pa (se dri bsnam pa) having that smell Pth.
vulgar for bsód-snyoms (bsod snyoms), alms.
Sch. dung-beetle.

wylie: se ba, gse ba, bse ba
english: 1. rose-bush, rose-tree; rose; ɣser-mdog-sé-ba-me (gser mdog se ba me) (for mé-tog (me tog)) Lt., prob. the yellow rose; wild roses with beautiful and rich blossoms frequently adorn the slopes of the lower hills in the Himalaya mountains; whether the se-rgód (se rgod) Med. and the 'wild rose' of Cs. are identical, seems to be questionable; *se-ḍúm* (*se dum*) C. hip, haw. - šiṅ-sé-ba (shing se ba) is mentioned as the food of the silk-worm Glr., hence = ó-se-šiṅ (o se shing). - 3. thorn?
gray, skra se-bo (skra se bo) gray hair; mgo se-bo (mgo se bo) (resp. dbu se-bo (dbu se bo)) a gray-headed person. - In col. language many things which we call gray, are styled white.
(C. *sen-ḍú* (*sen du*), W. *sem-rú* (*sem ru*)) pomegranate.
Mil. a kind of ornament, e.g. made of pearls.
C. the venereal disease; se-rmá (se rma) syphilitic ulcers Sch.
fig Med.
n. of a large monastery near Lhasa.
carrot W.
half open, *se-rél ċuṅ-ċe* (*se rel cung ce*) to open half (doors, lids, covers etc.) W.
Cs.: 'a tree or shrub, good for hedges, se-dúg (se dug) 1. poison contained in that shrub. 2. = se-móg (se mog) Cs.

obliquely, awry, sideways, ség-ycod-pa (seg ycod pa) Cs., *ség-ḍhe-la ḍhá-wa* (*seg dhe la dha wa*) C., *ség-ḍe-la ḍé-ċe* (*seg de la de ce*) W. to cut off obliquely (opp. to *ṭé-kaṅ-la* (*te kang la*) straight C.).
small stones, gravel W.
v. yseṅ (gseng).
W. *siṅ-ge* (*sing ge*), lion, séṅ-ge-mo (seng ge mo) lioness, séṅ-gei rál-pa (seng ge'i ral pa) the mane of
